And so really a foundation for being able to influence people through pram is sort of creating this movie of your own life that you then show to people in whole or in part for different purposes. You have scenes in the movie that you show to people for different purposes and you have made yourself a movie-like character that will resonate with your audience. The other thing that we'll come back to Hefner in a little bit because Hefner is very much about a philosophical position as much as he is anything else. One interesting thing if you page 16 he talks about the pot that taking up of the pipe, the top paragraph there, the best accessory, taking up of the pipe. It tells you a lot about affectation by design. The claim that is done unconsciously is bullshit and he actually tells you the sources that led to that particular device. I think he's a very strategic thinker. Page 17, Dr. Laura, who for a period of time was the highest paid and most influential radio personality in America. This little item here from a book by the way I did not get for you but you should all get it and read it. It's a book called Sham if you don't have it. And he got it about 80% right about the success industry. There's some stuff the author didn't quite understand but he got it about 80% right and so it's a worthwhile book for you to read. The Hefner book is Hefner's Little Black Book. I don't know. But here about Dr. Laura, here is her chosen personal narrative summarized as a statement. So we get to the core of what she wants known about her. And that is introducing herself each day on a program. She would say her name followed by and I am my kid's mom. And at the time of the start of the show it was a contrarian position to feminist positioning, to feminist wisdom. And it's a very clear positioning statement about what she's about aimed for acceptance, resonance with a certain audience. Obviously turning off a significant segment of the audience as well instantly. And so there you have personal narrative in I don't know five, six, seven words summarized for her. If you read, which you can read on your own time, everything about Dr. Laura you will find of course massive actual conflict with chosen personal narrative, reinvention of personal narrative on several occasions and you will see that none of that matters. On page 22 we get to our friend John Gray, men are from Mars, women are from Venus, one of the great high concepts of our time. Stole it of course from a common cliche and adopted as personal positioning. One of the most important things that you can pull from there is that personal narrative should include conveying upon yourself grand and great importance. That there is groundbreaking, life changing, radical and revolutionary things being done by this person. All self-proclamation. I mean nothing John Gray said was anything anybody didn't already know. He has a communication gap based on gender. Well big surprise there. But he made it sound like the epic discovery of the century was secrets possessed only by he. And so I've given you some John Gray stuff to read that his personal narrative related. Page 25 talks a little bit about one of John Gray's x y's, Barbara DeAngelois, the making love work gal. And Marianne Williamson, the new thought minister, speaker, author. And what's interesting there is that Barbara DeAngelois' position has a relationship guru. She has no background, no resume, no credentials, no anything to support that position. Most people don't remember but she was Doug Henning's stage assistant in his magic act. That's where she came from. And decided to be a relationship guru. And Marianne Williamson was a lounge singer singing in bars when she decided to be a new thought leader. And a very hard to swallow thing. But I think necessary to swallow is that these traditional credentials, this attempt to influence by resume and qualifications or concern about the lack thereof is completely irrelevant to influencing people. And again, if you want to look at the political arena, we've never elected the best qualified person by resume to be president. And if you were hiring them, everybody we've put in office, maybe with the exception of George W, who actually had some legitimate qualifications to be there, there's no reason to put anybody there. We put there based on their resumes. That's not, you know, that's not what gets them there. And the guys right now trying to run on their resumes are getting their head handed to them. It doesn't work. And so the fact that it doesn't work tells you that it is irrelevant. And so if you've got one, you got to be real careful not to be overly dependent on it or to give it more credit than it is due. And if you don't have one, you got to understand them make any difference. You make yourself up. And you're accepted in whatever way you make yourself up. Because for the most part, again, we're creating fictional characters. We're not doing nonfiction here, we're doing fiction. So we can make up any way we want to make them up. John could have come out of a cave with secret oriental strategies that nobody and if he said it enough, that would be fine. And it would never be questioned. And it would be infinitely more valuable, by the way, that if John had 14 degrees from five different universities in everything that has to do with exercise and fitness, because nobody gives a rat's ass about those degrees. They're just not persuasive. It's the story that is persuasive. And the story exists separate from your actual past credentials, etc. And if you look at one person after another, I gave you Susie Orman too, and some stuff about Susie. If you look at one person after another, you will find that they are inventions. And that really is the basis for influence is invention. I might point out it's sort of an aside, but pages 28 and 29 tell you something really useful about Susie Orman. Actually, all the way to page 30. Her success is based on creating female friendly language about money, a topic which typically the female gender is even more uncomfortable with than the male gender. And so she has sort of married money to feel good new thought language in a way that has made the whole topic much more significant. And so, like on page 30, there's two significant things about that that actually everybody does, who has great influence in print or verbally, is one they oversimplify what it is that they are presenting. People do not want complex ideas, increasingly so. And so that's not welcome. Again, the political example are the policy wonks who actually try and talk to the public about specifics. The public don't want to hear specifics. And so guys like Kerry and Gore are woefully handicapped. Clinton would have been without Carvel because they actually will give you the 45 minute dissertation of all of the details of their program. Well, nobody wants to hear that. And most people won't listen to it. So these people of influence in part through writing have all mastered, if you will, the art of oversimplification. To the point that they're grossly, they're highly criticized for it by experts in their respective fields, which by the way is another way you know you're getting it right.
